summary: Cathy O'Neil reviews Julia Angwin's book 'Dragnet Nation,' which chronicles Angwin's attempts to understand and escape the pervasive surveillance infrastructure tracking everyday citizens. O'Neil praises the book's detailed 'data audit' and Angwin's pragmatic approach to privacy—not seeking perfection but making trackers work harder. The review highlights how opting out of surveillance is nearly impossible even for a tech-savvy journalist willing to use burner phones and esoteric privacy services. O'Neil critiques the book for not fully exploring the aggregate societal implications of surveillance, though she endorses its educational value. She concludes that privacy is becoming commoditized—soon only the wealthy will be able to afford it.
key_points: Angwin's 'data audit' reveals how much personal data exists and how futile attempts to delete or opt out truly are
The surveillance industry operates without regard for public interest, as exemplified by the 'Wiretapper's Ball' selling tracking tech to governments
Staying off the grid requires either extreme sophistication or extreme wealth, making privacy increasingly a luxury good
The psychological toll of caring about surveillance in a world of 'blithe acceptance' pushes people toward paranoid 'data survivalism'
The critical unanswered question is whether any data trackers pursue the public interest at all, and what the long-term consequences are if none do
money_quotes: She doesn't want to hand herself over to them on a silver platter. That is already very very hard.
The key question is, who has access? and what do they do with it?
We don't really get into asking, 'Is in the public's interest?'
To what extent is the public's interest being pursued, if at all, by all of these data trackers and data miners?
You have to either be incredibly sophisticated or incredibly rich to get this done, at least right now.
My guess is that, in the future, it will be more about the latter category: privacy will be enjoyed only by those people who can afford it.
It is not a lot of fun to be the only person caring about the tracking in a world of blithe acceptance.
key_insight: Privacy is rapidly becoming a luxury that only the wealthy or technically sophisticated can afford, while the rest of society is left with no choice but blithe acceptance of pervasive surveillance.
